title: STUDIO Science — Why the Sky Is Blue

brand.title: STUDIO Science
brand.subtitle: Evidence-first explainers
brand.cta: Follow for the next phenomenon unpacked
brand.legal_line: Synthetic presenter · illustrative visualization labeled NOT TO SCALE

science_subject.key_measurement: Rayleigh scattering intensity scales as 1/λ⁴ — shorter wavelengths scatter more
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific visualization: sunlight entering Earth's atmosphere, blue shorter wavelengths scattering in all directions while red passes through at sunset angle; accurate color, neutral clinical grade, no decorative bloom

sources.citation: Lord Rayleigh, "On the scattering of light by small particles," Philosophical Magazine, 1871
NASA Earth Observatory — Why Is the Sky Blue?
Craig F. Bohren & Donald R. Huffman, Absorption and Scattering of Light by Small Particles (Wiley, 1983)

beats.speech_text: Look up on a clear day — the sky is blue. But sunlight is white. So where did the blue go?
Sunlight is all colors traveling together. Air molecules are tiny — smaller than the wavelength of visible light — so they scatter short blue waves in every direction.
Lord Rayleigh worked out the math in 1871: scatter intensity rises sharply as wavelength shrinks — roughly one over lambda to the fourth power.
Watch the payoff: blue light ricochets through the atmosphere toward your eyes from every direction — while redder light sails through at low sun angles, painting sunsets.
beats.visualization_prompt: Rayleigh scattering diagram: white sunbeam entering atmosphere, blue photons scattering omnidirectionally, red-orange beam passing through at grazing angle; clinical accurate color

beats.speech_text: One simple scattering law explains the color above you, the blush at dusk, and why distant mountains look hazy blue. Physics hiding in plain sight.
